coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots as multifunctional additives in zinc-ion batteries: progress, challenges, and opportunities

coredata.dc:description: For next-generation energy storage systems (ESSs), carbon dots (CDs) have emerged as revolutionary nanomaterials. CDs offer exceptional structural versatility and unique physicochemical properties. This review critically examines the transformative role of CDs in zinc-ion batteries (ZIBs). Recent advances have demonstrated remarkable improvements in battery performance, including enhanced cycling stability (>90 % capacity retention over 1000 cycles), superior rate capability (5 A g −1), and effective dendrite suppression. Herein, we systematically analyze the fundamental aspects of CDs, from size-controlled synthesis methods (2–10 nm) to their distinctive properties, including tunable surface chemistry, quantum confinement effects, and high electrical conductivity (>100 S cm−1). CDs can enhance ZIB performance via multiple mechanisms: namely, dendrite suppressors in anodes, conductivity enhancers in cathodes, electrolyte modifiers for stable ion transport, and functionalized separators for uniform zinc (Zn) deposition. Our critical analysis reveals that CD-modified ZIBs achieve significantly improved performance metrics, including higher specific capacities (>400 mAh g−1), reduced voltage polarization (<100 mV), and enhanced rate performance (>80 % capacity retention at 10C). We also address current challenges in CDs synthesis and integration, including scalability, cost-effectiveness, and long-term stability. Emerging research directions, such as smart responsive CDs and hybrid architectures are further highlighted. This work provides strategic insights for researchers and engineers working towards commercial-scale, high-performance ZIBs, offering a roadmap for sustainable energy storage solutions.

row.entry: Quasi-spherical nanoparticles, typically < 10 nm, with a crystalline core
Exhibit fluorescence that varies with size; possess tunable surface groups. (–OH, –COOH, –NH2)
Utilized in bioimaging, photocatalysis, and sensor technology
Small fragments of graphene, measuring 1–10 nm, made up of sp 2-hybridized carbon atoms
Feature high electrical conductivity and distinctive optical characteristics
Applied in energy storage, solar cells, and electrocatalytic processes
Similar in size to CQDs but primarily amorphous or partially crystalline
Their fluorescence arises from surface states rather than quantum effects; possess abundant surface functional groups
Employed in sensing, catalytic activities, and bioimaging applications
Formed from carbon-based polymers, typically have an amorphous structure
Display fluorescence that can be adjusted depending on the polymer precursor; highly biocompatible
Commonly used for drug delivery, bioimaging, and biosensing applications

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: For next-generation energy storage systems (ESSs), carbon dots (CDs) have emerged as revolutionary nanomaterials. CDs offer exceptional structural versatility and unique physicochemical properties. This review critically examines the transformative role of CDs in zinc-ion batteries (ZIBs). Recent advances have demonstrated remarkable improvements in battery performance, including enhanced cycling stability (>90 % capacity retention over 1000 cycles), superior rate capability (5 A g −1), and effective dendrite suppression. Herein, we systematically analyze the fundamental aspects of CDs, from size-controlled synthesis methods (2–10 nm) to their distinctive properties, including tunable surface chemistry, quantum confinement effects, and high electrical conductivity (>100 S cm−1). CDs can enhance ZIB performance via multiple mechanisms: namely, dendrite suppressors in anodes, conductivity enhancers in cathodes, electrolyte modifiers for stable ion transport, and functionalized separators for uniform zinc (Zn) deposition. Our critical analysis reveals that CD-modified ZIBs achieve significantly improved performance metrics, including higher specific capacities (>400 mAh g−1), reduced voltage polarization (<100 mV), and enhanced rate performance (>80 % capacity retention at 10C). We also address current challenges in CDs synthesis and integration, including scalability, cost-effectiveness, and long-term stability. Emerging research directions, such as smart responsive CDs and hybrid architectures are further highlighted. This work provides strategic insights for researchers and engineers working towards commercial-scale, high-performance ZIBs, offering a roadmap for sustainable energy storage solutions.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Of late, with the global transition towards renewable energy, the urgent need for efficient and sustainable energy storage solutions has intensified [1,2]. Presently, lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) dominate the commercial market [3]. However, persistent challenges, including limited lithium resources, safety concerns, and environmental impacts have driven exploration of alternative systems based on earth-abundant elements [4–6]. Among these alternatives, zinc-ion batteries (ZIBs) have emerged as particularly promising candidates [7,8]. With their high theoretical capacity (820 mAh g−1), low redox potential (−0.76 V vs. SHE), abundant Zn resources, and enhanced safety through aqueous electrolytes [9], ZIBs show considerable potential as successors to LIBs, especially for grid-scale storage applications [10]. Fundamental challenges remain: Zn’s higher atomic weight plus lower electrochemical potential result in reduced energy density compared to lithium-based systems [11,12]. Conventional cathode materials such as manganese oxides [13] and vanadium oxides [14,15] are seen to exhibit lower specific capacities than their LIB counterparts [16]. To address these issues, recent advances in material engineering have demonstrated significant progress.

In ZIBs, Zn2+ ions migrate between the cathode (positive electrode) and the anode (negative electrode), separated by a conductive aqueous electrolyte and a porous separator. ZIBs operate through an electrochemical redox process, converting chemical energy into electrical energy. The predominant energy storage mechanism is the Zn2+ insertion/extraction process, similar to the intercalation mechanisms observed in lithium-ion and sodium-ion batteries [17,18].

Similar to lithium-ion batteries (LIBs), the cathode in ZIBs functions as an intercalation host, allowing the reversible incorporation and release of Zn2+ ions during charge–discharge cycles. However, unlike LIBs, which typically use graphite as the anode, ZIBs employ metallic zinc, enabling a reversible plating and stripping process rather than relying on a secondary intercalation material. The use of metallic zinc as the anode offers significant advantages, including cost-effectiveness, stability in aqueous environments, and superior energy storage capacity. Zinc exhibits a specific (gravimetric) capacity of 820 mAh g−1 and a volumetric capacity of 5,845 mAh cm−3, significantly higher than those of graphite, which has specific and volumetric capacities of 372 mAh g−1 and 841 mAh cm−3, respectively. This highlights the superior energy storage potential of zinc over graphite in terms of both mass and volume. The general working mechanism of ZIBs can be summarized as follows.

Researchers have explored multiple strategies, including Zn anode surface modification [19–22], electrolyte engineering [23–25], and innovative cathode material design [16,26]. The development of suitable anode materials for reversible Zn plating and stripping has proved to be particularly crucial for advancing ZIB technology [27]. Despite these improvements, challenges such as active material dissolution and manufacturing validation continue to impede widespread commercialization [10]. It is noted that Zn-based systems maintain significant advantages in material recyclability and environmental impact [28].

In Fig. 1 , researchers have extensively explored multidimensional carbon nanomaterials for both cathode and anode applications. Such investigations have revealed promising pathways for enhancing ZIB performance through strategic material design and interface engineering. Various carbon-based architectures, from one-dimensional nanotubes to three-dimensional frameworks, have demonstrated unique capabilities in improving conductivity, stability, and ion transport properties.

In this context, carbon dots (CDs) have emerged as a promising class of materials that can tackle many persistent challenges in ZIBs development. These nanoscale carbon materials offer distinct advantages over conventional semiconductor quantum dots, including lower toxicity and reduced environmental impact [43]. Their exceptional chemical stability, design flexibility, and satisfactory electrochemical activity have enabled diverse applications across ESSs, electrocatalysis, and biomedicine. Herein, synthesis methods, material properties, performance enhancement mechanisms, and integration strategies across various battery components are highlighted.

It is observed that applications in lithium-ion [44–46], sodium-ion batteries, supercapacitors (SCs) [47] and their potential in ZIB systems have largely been unexplored. This review addresses this knowledge gap by providing a comprehensive examination of CD applications in ZIBs. The unique characteristics of CDs make them particularly suitable for ZIB applications. Their nanoscale dimensions (2–10 nm) create exceptionally high surface-area-to-volume ratios. CDs have diverse surface chemistries that can enable precise control over electrode–electrolyte interactions. Moreover, unlike larger carbon structures, CDs can be synthesized through environmentally benign processes, using renewable precursors, aligning with the inherent sustainability advantages of Zn-based ESSs.

In addressing key challenges in ZIB technology, through systematic analysis of recent advancements and emerging trends, this review illuminates the transformative potential of CDs. We examine their multifaceted roles across various battery components, analyze fundamental enhancement mechanisms, and identify critical areas for future research and development. This wide-ranging assessment provides valuable insights for researchers and engineers working towards next-generation ESSs, offering a roadmap for advancing ZIB technology through strategic integration of CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Carbon dots: synthesis, properties, and characterization
ce:section.ce:para: The serendipitous discovery of CDs during single-walled carbon nanotube purification [48] has initiated extensive research into their synthesis mechanisms [49] and unique structural properties. These zero-dimensional nanoparticles, typically below 10 nm in diameter, feature distinctive core–shell architectures where graphitic cores are surrounded by versatile functional groups (–OH, –NH2, –COOH), as depicted in Fig. 2 . The unique structural arrangement confers exceptional properties, particularly high surface-area-to-volume ratio and enhanced electrical conductivity through efficient electron-hole pair generation. CDs display remarkable quantum confinement effects that contribute to their distinctive optoelectronic properties [50]. These characteristics, combined with straightforward and cost-effective synthetic methods, establish CDs as promising alternatives to conventional carbon nanomaterials like fullerenes, nanodiamonds, and carbon nanotubes.

In Table 1
, based on structural characteristics and applications, CDs are classified into four categories: graphene quantum dots (GQDs), carbon quantum dots (CQDs), carbon nanodots (CNDs), and polymer dots (PDs). While sharing common features, each type exhibits specific properties suited for different applications, from SCs [51] and solar cells [52] to various batteries [53,54].

In Fig. 3
a, CD synthesis follows two strategic approaches: top-down and bottom-up methods [55]. Top-down approaches systematically break down larger carbon structures into nanoscale dots. Bottom-up methods construct CDs from molecular precursors and organic natural resources. Real versatility emerges in the ability to modify surface properties through heteroatom doping, functionalization, and surface passivation. Bottom-up approaches face challenges in controlling the formation of side products, thus requiring additional purification steps [56]. Such approaches offer unique benefits through straightforward one-pot surface modifications and diverse structural possibilities. The development of scalable synthesis methods remains critical for broader adoption [57,58].

The synthesis of CDs, through top-down methods, is seen to harness mechanical, thermal, or electrical energy to transform bulk carbon materials into nanoscale structures. As illustrated in Fig. 3 a, these approaches utilize diverse starting materials, ranging from carbon rods [59], carbon fibers [60], carbon black [61], carbon nanotubes [62], activated carbon [63], and graphene oxide [64,65].

The arc discharge method, which led to the serendipitous discovery of CDs during carbon nanotube purification, produces strongly fluorescent nanostructures [48]. Although this technique yields high-purity materials, challenges in particle size distribution control and industrial scalability limit its widespread adoption. Laser ablation offers exceptional control over particle morphology through precise manipulation of laser pulse parameters [66]. Research has shown that longer pulse widths facilitate more effective nanostructure production and size regulation [67]. This method generates high-purity products. However, the requirement for sophisticated equipment and relatively low production volumes presents shortcomings. Electrochemical exfoliation, first demonstrated using carbon nanotubes [68], provides a scalable route toward industrial production. This approach employs electrical energy to break down bulk carbons into nanoscale dots. Although cost-effective, such a process introduces structural defects that necessitate additional purification steps.

It is noted that the ultrasonic synthesis method utilizes alternating high and low-pressure waves to fragment carbon materials through cavitation bubbles. Notable achievements include the synthesis of graphene quantum dots within 4 h, demonstrating 27.8 % quantum yield (QY) without acid treatment [69]. This technique successfully combines rapid processing with effective size control. Chemical oxidation with activated carbon produces multicolor photoluminescent CDs through oxidative treatment with strong acids [63]. This method strikes a balance between reasonable yields and straightforward processing requirements. Despite their advantages in large-scale production, top-down methods share common challenges: extended processing times, harsh reaction conditions, and dependence on expensive equipment. In Table 2
, a comprehensive comparison of these approaches is listed, highlighting their relative strengths and limitations in terms of size control and QY optimization.

The synthesis of CDs through bottom-up approaches represents an elegant strategy that constructs these nanostructures from molecular precursors. At its core, this method relies on both the carbonization and polymerization of small organic molecules [79], creating carbon-based nanostructures through various molecular interactions, including covalent and non-covalent bonds [83]. This process offers remarkable flexibility in functionalizing the resulting materials, enabling precise control over their properties for specific applications [84].

In typical synthesis protocols, simple organic molecules and polymers serve as starting materials. Common precursors include glucose, sucrose, and citric acid [85]. As shown in Fig. 3 b, these precursors undergo a complex transformation process, where sequential carbonization reactions lead to the formation of CDs through nucleation, growth, and surface passivation stages [86,87].

As a cornerstone approach in bottom-up synthesis, the hydrothermal method is of prime importance. This technique gained prominence through pioneering work, which highlighted the production of fluorescent CDs from l-ascorbic acid. A 4 h treatment at 180 °C yielded uniform particles measuring 2 nm in diameter [88]. The method's elegance lies in its simplicity, eliminating the need for expensive chemicals or complex surface treatments. The hydrothermal method offers heightened control over particle dimensions and surface chemistry. However, in scaling up production, there are limitations. It is observed that the related solvothermal approach requires longer processing times.

Pyrolysis represents another significant bottom-up strategy. First developed for CD synthesis, pyrolysis involves the thermal decomposition of organic precursors under inert conditions [89]. Its versatility became evident when researchers successfully converted watermelon peels into high-quality fluorescent CDs, demonstrating its potential for vaporizing biomass waste [90].

The microwave-assisted synthesis method introduced a rapid and efficient production route. Initial studies achieved particles ∼ 3.7 nm in size, greatly reducing reaction times compared to conventional thermal approaches [86]. The carbonization method provides another versatile pathway, producing high-quantum-yield CDs through controlled incomplete combustion of carbon-rich precursors [79].

The template method has emerged as a sophisticated approach for achieving precise size control. Through careful thermal decomposition and template removal, researchers produced CDs with remarkably consistent dimensions (3.3 ± 0.9 nm) [91]. In general, bottom-up methods can provide superior control over chemical composition and structural quality. However, such methods require careful optimization to maintain consistent particle size and formation parameters.

The performance of CDs in battery applications stems from an intricate interplay of their structural and chemical characteristics. From morphology and atomic composition to interfacial characteristics, these properties can be fine-tuned through synthesis methods and post-processing conditions. As a fundamental parameter, particle size is an important physical property that can affect the appearance of materials and can directly influence critical aspects, such as diffusion length, electronic structure, and atomic configuration. Beyond size control, the introduction of heteroatoms through doping processes can transform basic carbon–oxygen frameworks into more complex structures. This doping strategy creates additional active sites, expands interlayer spacing, and is seen to enhance electronic conductivity.

In modulating interfacial and electrochemical behaviors, surface chemistry particularly plays a pivotal role. For example, amino groups contribute to improved pseudo-capacitance. As such, hydroxyl and carboxyl groups highly influence electrode–electrolyte interactions through their impact on hydrophobic or hydrophilic characteristics [92]. The strategic modification of these surface functional groups hugely impacts ion deposition processes in battery applications.

It is acknowledged that precise control over CD size and morphology can be achieved through careful manipulation of synthesis parameters and purification methods. For instance, Ding et al. [93] exercised such control through a one-step solvothermal process. In Fig. 4
a, precursors (l-glutamic acid and o-phenylenediamine) along with various solvents were used. Hence, it is evident that by altering solvent ratios and types, CD properties can be directly influenced, particularly their fluorescence characteristics [94].

Post-synthesis purification emerges as an essential step in CD preparation, employing techniques such as dialysis [95], ultra-purification [96], and column chromatography. These purification methods not only ensure precise size control but also enable effective separation of CD mixtures. In Fig. 4 b and c, the importance of synthesis conditions in controlling surface properties is observed. Both Mao et al. [92] and Wu et al. [97] demonstrated that CD hydrophilicity or hydrophobicity can be systematically modulated through careful selection of precursors and solvent ratios.

In Fig. 4 d, the work of Yan et al. [98] states that column chromatography enabled the separation of water-soluble CDs into four distinct fractions, each with uniform size distribution. The significance of such precise size control extends beyond basic characterization. Yang et al. [99], for instance, have documented the remarkable effectiveness of hydrophilic CDs in suppressing Zn dendrite growth and mitigating water decomposition in aqueous Zn batteries.

Recent advances in nanomaterial science have witnessed the emergence of diverse materials, including hybrid structures, carbon allotropes, and various forms of CDs. Surface modification of these materials has become indispensable for enhancing key properties, such as hydrophobicity/hydrophilicity, corrosion resistance, dispersibility, and solubility. As illustrated in Fig. 5
a, these modifications are achieved through both covalent and non-covalent interactions [100,101], with their effectiveness influenced by multiple factors: namely, surfactant choice, surface functional groups, preparation methods, pH, and temperature. Such surface engineering strategically introduces specific chemical groups to optimize material properties for targeted applications [50,102].

Surface modification strategies for CDs fall into two main categories: covalent and non-covalent functionalization. Covalent functionalization involves forming strong chemical bonds between CDs and modifying agents, typically attaching carboxyl, hydroxyl, or amino groups to the CD surface. This approach provides enhanced stability and stronger interactions, making it ideal for applications requiring long-term durability. Common covalent modification methods include amidation [103], silylation [104], esterification [105], sulfonation [106,107], copolymerization [57], and Schiff's base reactions for introducing various functional groups or attaching larger molecules.

Non-covalent functionalization, conversely, relies on weaker interactions, such as electrostatic forces [108], van der Waals forces, hydrogen bonding, and π-π stacking [109], as well as complexation [110]. It is seen that this approach preserves the CD core structure, offering easier modification and reversibility. Such a perspective is particularly valuable for applications, requiring dynamic interactions. Examples include polymer adsorption and biomolecule attachment through electrostatic or π-π stacking interactions [83].

The surface chemistry of CDs plays a pivotal role in their electrochemical performance. Their abundant surface functional groups, particularly hydroxyl and carboxyl moieties, not only provide active sites but also enhance wettability and facilitate electrolyte ion adsorption, leading to efficient electric double layer (EDL) formation [50]. These groups serve different functions depending on the electrolyte environment. In alkaline conditions, carbonyl and carboxyl groups enhance pseudo-capacitance, while carbonyl and quinone groups are more effective in acidic environments. It is significant that surface functionalization with CD can influence the morphology of metal ions and particle size, offering additional control over material properties [111].

In UV–vis spectroscopy, CDs exhibit distinctive absorption characteristics, with prominent features between 240 and 350 nm. The absorption spectrum exhibits two characteristic peaks: one in the 240–280 nm range, corresponding to π–π* transitions within the aromatic C=C framework [115], and a broader peak around 350 nm, attributed to n–π* transitions, arising from surface functional groups [116]. The intensity of these transitions provides insight into the material's structure. Stronger π–π* transitions indicate well-developed graphitic domains with extended conjugation. Pronounced n–π* transitions suggest abundant surface functionalization that influences the material's optical and chemical properties.

The photoluminescence (PL) behavior of CDs presents an intriguing contrast to conventional semiconductor quantum dots (QDs). While both materials exhibit size-dependent properties, CDs' emission characteristics are primarily determined by their surface chemistry rather than quantum confinement effects [117]. This fundamental difference is clearly seen in their emission profiles: most CDs produce blue to green emissions, though some variants achieve longer wavelengths [118]. The enhanced photoluminescence arises from multiple mechanisms, including surface-state emissions, core-surface electronic interactions, and the presence of molecular fluorophores. Notably, the QY of CDs continues to improve through advances in synthesis methods and surface modification strategies, reflecting the ongoing optimization of their optical properties.

The electronic properties of CDs are linked to their core structure. Such properties can range from amorphous to highly crystalline, depending on the synthesis approach. In Fig. 5 b, bottom-up methods like carbonization and hydrothermal synthesis typically produce more amorphous structures; top-down approaches such as laser ablation and graphitization yield highly crystalline CDs [119]. These structural differences greatly influence their properties. Hence, amorphous CDs exhibit diverse size distributions and surface functionalities. In contrast, crystalline CDs demonstrate superior structural stability, electrical conductivity, and photoluminescence. The degree of crystallinity critically impacts key performance metrics, including charge carrier mobility, photoluminescence QY, and catalytic activity [120].

CDs have a unique core–shell architecture. Their electronic character arises from when its carbon core is encapsulated by surface functional groups. This carbon core exhibits both sp 2 (graphitic) and sp 3 (amorphous) hybridization states, which fundamentally determine their electronic properties and energy storage performance [121]. During redox reactions, the sp 2-rich domains with their graphitic structure facilitate efficient electron transfer. Due to their amorphous nature, the sp 3-rich regions contribute to structural stability. This dual character makes CDs particularly valuable for energy storage applications, including lithium-ion [46] and metal-air batteries [122].

Hybridization states play distinct but complementary roles in ESSs. The sp 2 hybridized regions, characterized by delocalized π-electrons, are seen to enhance electrical conductivity and charge transfer capabilities. Such sp 2 hybridized regions are paramount for electrode performance in batteries and SCs [123]. Simultaneously, the sp 3 hybridized regions provide structural stability, contributing to extended battery cycle life [124]. The optimal balance between these hybridization states is, therefore, vital for achieving both high conductivity and long-term stability in energy storage applications.

The exceptional electrochemical properties of CDs stem from their unique combination of high specific surface area, abundant active sites, and superior conductivity. A landmark study of size-dependent electrochemical performance in graphene quantum dots reveals an inverse relationship between particle size and specific capacitance; the smallest GQDs maintained optimal power response even at extremely high scan rates of 5000 V s−1, a crucial finding for high-performance microelectronic applications [125].

Recent advances in CD modification have further expanded their electrochemical capabilities. Nitrogen doping is seen to greatly assist CD performance through the introduction of additional active sites and pseudocapacitive groups, leading to improved capacitance and cycling stability in SC applications [126]. These modified CDs amplify versatility beyond energy storage, finding applications in sensing technologies where precise control of electron transfer is paramount [127].

The strategic incorporation of heteroatoms into CDs fundamentally alters their electronic and structural properties via modifications of electronic energy thresholds, Fermi levels, and localized electronic configurations [113,128]. Such alterations yield increased electrode performance through multiple mechanisms i.e. increased active site availability, improved conductivity, and enhanced chemical reactivity [129]. In Fig. 5 c, various heteroatoms (N, S, P, B) can be introduced to modify electron distribution patterns, hugely augmenting the electrocatalytic capabilities of CD-enhanced materials [130].

Further, studies have demonstrated the profound impact of specific dopants. In 2020, Stachurski et al. [131] showed how nitrogen doping systematically shifts oxidation potentials in proportion to dopant concentration. Building on this concept, Kim & Ahn [132] successfully developed high-performance lithium-ion battery anodes, using nitrogen and boron co-doped CQDs. The effectiveness of nitrogen doping is particularly evident in N-CQDs, which exhibit remarkably refined fluorescence emission with QY, reaching 73.10 % under optimized conditions [133,134]. Beyond optical properties, heteroatom doping also boosts electrochemical performance by creating additional active sites and optimizing diffusion pathways, leading to better specific capacity, rate performance, and solid-electrolyte interphase (SEI) layer stability [135,136].

To evaluate CD optimal efficiency, QY is seen to serve as a critical metric, for measuring the conversion of absorbed light into fluorescence [114]. In general, while higher QY values generally indicate greater performance in sensing and light-emitting applications, this parameter is intricately linked to CD surface states and electronic structure [137]. In Fig. 5 d, the relationship between doping and QY plays a core role in optimizing both optical and electrochemical properties, positioning heteroatom-doped CDs as promising candidates for advanced sensing and energy storage applications.

Understanding the complex nature of CDs requires a comprehensive suite of analytical techniques. Each CD provide unique insights into their physical and chemical properties. These characterization methods collectively build a detailed picture of CD structure, composition, and behavior, essential for their diverse applications.

Electron microscopy techniques form the foundation of CD characterization. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) reveals fundamental aspects of morphology and size distribution, confirming the typical sub-10 nm dimensions of these materials. High-resolution TEM takes this analysis further, enabling detailed examination of crystallinity and lattice spacing, providing principal comparisons with graphitic carbon references.

Surface analysis techniques offer complementary insights. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) achieves remarkable precision in mapping surface topography, providing sub-nanometer resolution of particle thickness, aspect ratio, and layer structure. Raman spectroscopy exhibits structural ordering through its characteristic D and G bands. Here, the intensity ratio (ID/IG) serves as a key indicator of crystallization levels. X-ray diffraction (XRD) complements these techniques by identifying cluster-like surface structures, typically showing broad peaks around 2θ = ∼21° that correspond to graphite (002) planes.

Chemical composition analysis relies heavily on spectroscopic methods. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy identifies fundamental surface functional groups, including hydroxyl, carboxyl, carbonyl, and epoxy groups. FTIR spectroscopy also provides evidence of heteroatom doping. Moreover, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) can offer precise determination of elemental composition and chemical states, particularly valuable for monitoring processes of oxidation.

As for the optical and surface properties of CDs, they are probed through additional specialized techniques. UV–visible spectroscopy reveals their electronic structure through characteristic absorption peaks: π-π* transitions of C=C bonds at 220 nm and n-π* transitions of C=O bonds at 281 nm, with distinct modifications observed in doped variants such as N-CQDs. Zeta potential measurements provide indispensable information about colloidal stability and surface modifications by assessing the electrostatic repulsion between particles, helping predict and optimize their behavior in various environments. Overall, these analytical methods provide a comprehensive understanding of CD properties. For specific applications, from energy storage to sensing technologies, researchers can optimize their synthesis and modification.

Through careful manipulation of synthesis parameters, the remarkable versatility of CDs arises from their highly tunable properties, which researchers can precisely control. In Fig. 6 , several key factors work in concert to determine the final characteristics of these fascinating nanomaterials. Most fundamental, perhaps, is the choice of carbon source or precursor. When researchers use citric acid as a starting material, for instance, they typically obtain highly fluorescent CDs. Other common precursors include glucose, ethylene glycol, and various aromatic compounds, as shown in Fig. 6 a. The functional groups naturally present in these precursor molecules leave their mark on the resulting CDs, influencing both surface chemistry and optical properties [138]. This understanding allows scientists to strategically select and modify precursor structures to synthesize carbon polymer dots (CPDs) with characteristics optimized for specific applications.

The conditions under which these precursors transform into CDs also play an essential role in modifying precursor structures. To determine the degree of carbonization and the size of the resultant particles, temperature and reaction time work together. Generally, higher temperatures and longer reaction times yield larger CDs with increased graphitization. As the reaction progresses, researchers observe that QY may either increase or decrease, depending on the specific synthesis conditions and starting materials. These changes reflect evolving electronic structures and energy levels, as demonstrated in Fig. 6 b [139–141].

The pH of the reaction medium adds another layer of control over CD properties. In Fig. 6 c, acidic conditions tend to promote the formation of carboxylic acid functional groups on the CD surface: basic conditions favor the development of amine groups. These pH-dependent surface modifications greatly influence CDs' solubility, reactivity, and photoluminescence properties. By carefully adjusting pH, researchers can fine-tune the electronic structure and emission behavior of CDs for specific applications in sensing, imaging, and catalysis [142,143].

The choice of solvent in solvothermal synthesis provides yet another powerful tool for shaping CD characteristics. Polar solvents like water and ethanol tend to produce spherical CDs. Non-polar solvents, however, such as toluene often result in irregular morphologies. In Fig. 6 d, increasing solvent polarity is seen to lead to fascinating changes in the photoluminescence properties of the resulting CDs i.e. a redshift in emission, a broadening of the emission bandwidth plus a reduction in QY [144,145]. This solvent-dependent control enables researchers to precisely tune CD structures for their intended applications.

Post-synthesis treatments play a prime role in determining the final properties of CDs. Reaction products typically contain a mix of various CD fractions, molecular intermediates, and additional byproducts. Synthesis from waste or biomass sources often yields impurities that can influence the optical emission characteristics of CDs. These byproducts and residual compounds may alter fluorescent properties, potentially impacting performance in applications where consistent emission is essential.

A comprehensive understanding of synthesis parameters and their influence on CD properties is pivotal for optimizing their performance in ZIBs. Factors such as precursor selection, reaction temperature, pH, and post-synthesis treatments vastly impact the structural, optical, and electrochemical properties of CDs. To stabilize the electrolyte interface, suppress dendrite formation, and facilitate ion transport within ZIBs, researchers can fine-tune the size, surface functionality, and electronic structure of CDs.

CDs have emerged as transformative materials in electrochemical energy storage. CDs have a high surface area, abundant active sites, enhanced conductivity, and superior electrode fabrication properties. Their integration into ZIBs represents a strategic approach to addressing fundamental performance challenges.

In the following sections, we explore how CDs enhance each major battery component, from stabilizing anodes and improving cathode performance to optimizing electrolytes and enhancing separator functionality. These improvements are seen to drive the development of next-generation ZIBs with higher energy density, extended cycle life, and improved safety profiles. In Table 3 , CD-enhanced ZIBs demonstrate impressive performance metrics across various battery components. Such ZIBs showcase their potential for applications, ranging from portable electronics to grid-scale energy storage.

Through systematic analysis of CD applications in ZIBs, we examine both current achievements and future opportunities in sustainable energy storage technology. This investigation reveals how strategic integration of CDs is reshaping our approach to battery design and performance optimization.

During Zn deposition, the promise of Zn metal anodes, with their impressive volumetric capacity, has long been tempered by two critical challenges: the formation of dendrites and parasitic interfacial reactions. These issues highly compromise battery performance. As a result, CDs have a shortened cycle life and reduced coulombic efficiency (CE) through persistent dendrite growth. Further, unwanted byproducts like hydrogen gas and inactive ZnO also inhibit their performance. To improve overall performance and longevity of ZIBs, recent research has focused intensively on improving Zn anode stability [164,165]. In this landscape, CDs have emerged as remarkably effective solutions through multiple mechanisms.

In addressing dendrite formation, a breakthrough approach involves engineering functional interfacial layers that optimize Zn-electrolyte contact [166]. Beyond traditional SEI applications, these protective layers serve dual purposes: stabilizing the Zn anode while regulating Zn deposition patterns. Notably, such layers immobilize water molecules, preventing parasitic reactions at the Zn electrode surface [167]. This strategy leverages dense, insoluble coating layers enriched with CD functional groups to achieve three key objectives: mitigating interfacial degradation, suppressing dendrite formation, and enhancing electrochemical stability.

Zhang et al. [146] demonstrated this concept's effectiveness by developing a coating comprising pristine CDs mixed with CMC binder for Zn anodes (Fig. 7
a). Their hydrophobic CD coating created uniform nucleation sites for even Zn deposition. Across the anode surface, local current density fluctuations are seen to decrease, and homogeneous electric field distribution is promoted. To study Zn plating behavior in symmetric cells, in-situ optical microscopy was used. In Fig. 7 b-c, the CD layer effectively reduced Zn2+ ion concentration polarization by neutralizing the “tip effect”. Results proved to be most striking. It is seen that, after only 10 h, the bare Zn anodes failed, revealing many surface irregularities. However, CD-coated anodes operated stably for over 40 h without any dendrite being formed. This dramatic improvement arose from increased Zn reaction kinetics, lower nucleation energy barriers, and controlled two-dimensional diffusion patterns.

CDs effectively enhance ZIBs by facilitating the formation of stable SEI, creating protective layers that regulate Zn ion transport while mitigating water-induced side reactions in aqueous systems. Ge et al. [148] demonstrated such advancement through the development of fluorine-doped CDs (F-CDs) as a protective layer for pristine Zn anodes. In Fig. 7 d, density functional theory (DFT) calculations reveal the strong zincophilic nature of F-CDs, exhibiting a negative adsorption energy of −0.43 eV toward Zn2+ ions. This F-CD layer promotes Zn (002) plane deposition. At the same time, hydrogen evolution and Zn corrosion is suppressed, achieving 99.32 % CE over 1100 h.

Electron probe microanalysis (EPMA) confirmed the layer's efficiency. After 15 cycles, the F-CD-protected anode maintained better stability than the bare Zn anodes (69.1 % Zn concentration compared to initial 85.0 %). In Fig. 7 e, the F-CDs layer further suppressed Zn hydrosulfide formation, reducing sulfur concentration from 4.8 % to 0.69 %. These results demonstrate the potential of F-CD protective layers in developing stable, high-performance Zn anodes for next-generation batteries.

In the architecture of ZIBs, cathode materials serve as fundamental determinants of both open circuit voltage (OCV) and capacity performance [168]. A select group of materials: namely, Prussian blue analogs, vanadium-based compounds, and manganese-based materials have emerged as promising candidates for reversible Zn intercalation and de-intercalation [26]. However, conventional cathodes face persistent challenges of limited capacity utilization and restricted electrochemically active surface areas. To overcome these limitations, structural engineering at mesoscopic and microscale levels is seen to be an effective strategy. In this approach, nanostructured electrode architectures augment ionic and electronic transport kinetics. Ultimately, both charge storage capacity and cycling stability in ZIBs are improved. Such architectural refinements represent an essential pathway toward realizing high-performance ZIBs.

Prussian blue analogs (PBAs), particularly manganese hexacyanoferrate (MnHCF), represent promising cathode materials for ZIBs. However, during cycling, PBAs face inherent limitations such as poor electrical conductivity and structural degradation. In their investigation, Xue et al. [158] addressed these challenges by synthesizing nitrogen-doped CDs (NCDs) integrated with MnHCF (Fig. 8
a-b). The researchers reveal how NCDs can enhance electrical conductivity through nitrogen's high electronegativity and electron-withdrawing effects. As a result, by inducing positive charge in neighboring carbon atoms, electron transport is facilitated.

In Fig. 8 c, the nitrogen doping strategy proves particularly effective by introducing structural defects that serve as additional electrochemically active sites for Zn ion insertion. Thus, performance is enhanced and manifests itself in larger redox peaks and increased specific capacities of the CD-modified MnHCF composite. This synergistic interaction between NCDs and MnHCF yields remarkable cycling stability, maintaining 91 % capacity retention after 1000 cycles at 1000 mA g−1. The effectiveness of CD modification, in addressing the conductivity challenges of PBA cathodes, is amply demonstrated.

CDs serve as important structural stabilizers in cathode materials, preventing active material dissolution. During cycling, their structural integrity is maintained. Besides, their mechanical properties help accommodate volume changes and mitigate structural strain during charge–discharge processes.

Wu et al. [41] verified this effectiveness through a composite of holey graphene (HG) and CQD-coated VO2 cathodes. The composite achieved remarkable cycling stability, retaining 80.1 % capacity over 1000 cycles, compared to merely 40.28 % for uncoated VO2, after 450 cycles. In Fig. 8 d and e, the solvothermal synthesis created a structure where CQDs act as “bridges,” establishing robust interfaces with HG, thus facilitating both electron and ion transport. It is noted that the integration of CQDs not only increased active sites but also effectively managed volume changes, resulting in an impressive initial discharge capacity of 443 mAh g−1, more than double that of pure VO2 (199 mAh g−1). This superb performance confirms how strategic incorporation of CDs can simultaneously address multiple challenges in cathode design, from structural stability to electrochemical performance.

For active site generation, it is observed that most studies focus on CD-mediated morphological control. Zhang et al. [160] proposed an innovative mechanism, addressing structure collapse and active material dissolution through a self-assembled CD-Cu2O core–shell architecture (Fig. 9
a). In their investigation, a fascinating interplay between structural and electronic properties is revealed.

DFT calculations signify that CD incorporation facilitates electron transfer to Cu, generating a more uniform and widespread electron cloud distribution (Fig. 9 b-d). This electronic restructuring is seen to improve both charge distribution and Zn2+ transport capabilities. CD modification can reduce Zn2+ adsorption energy, creating lower diffusion barriers in Cu2O-CDs-20 composites (Fig. 9 e). Subsequently, ion transport is facilitated, thereby enabling deeper penetration of Zn2+ ions; within the material's interior structure, storage sites are activated.

In Fig. 9 f, galvanostatic charge/discharge tests validate this mechanism, exhibiting much higher reversible capacities compared to CD-free analogues. This work shows how strategic CD incorporation can simultaneously address structural stability and enhance electrochemical performance through optimized charge transport and active site accessibility.

AZIBs face significant electrolyte-related challenges, including cathode dissolution, by-product formation, and anode-side dendrite growth [27]. While solid electrolytes offer potential solutions, their complex fabrication and lower ionic conductivity limit practical applications [170].

Zhang et al. [169] implemented an innovative “water structure regulation” strategy by adding CDs to conventional ZnSO4 electrolyte. Such a strategy was designed to mitigate water-induced parasitic reactions commonly observed at Zn anodes (Fig. 9 g). Both ex-situ and in-situ (SERS) surfaces enhanced Raman spectroscopy, revealing that the behavior of sulfate ions (SO4 2-) differed between the two electrolytes (Fig. 9 h-j). In Fig. 9k and l, DFT calculations disclosed that the dissociation energy of solvated H2O (−9.79 eV) is greater than symmetric H2O (−1.093 eV) and isolated H2O (−0.611 eV). This hierarchy arises from cation interactions with H2O molecules, which polarize O–H bonds, amplifying the reactivity of solvated H2O. Interfacial H2O regulation is vital for optimizing electrochemical performance. By adapting the above properties of CDs and their integration into the electrolyte, researchers can continue to develop innovative solutions for improving the performance and reliability of ZIBs.

Through their ability to modify Zn2+ ion solvation structures, CDs are competent alternatives as electrolyte additives. CDs surface functional groups are seen to facilitate hydrogen bonding with electrolyte molecules, creating optimized ion migration pathways and reducing transport resistance. This molecular-level engineering approach can increase cathode capacity and cycling stability [171,172]. CD incorporation can address multiple electrolyte challenges while improving overall battery performance. By modifying Zn ion solvation structures, reducing de-solvation energy barriers at electrode interfaces, and creating ordered ion transport channels, CDs can augment ZIB performance through multiple mechanisms.

Wang et al. [150] emphasized this effectiveness by functionalizing CQDs in ZnSO4 electrolyte (Fig. 10
a). The hybrid electrolyte's anionic groups are seen to promote strong Zn2 + absorption, forming solvated clusters [FCQDs Znx (H2O)y]2x+. These clusters expedited uniform Zn deposition and reduced de-solvation energy barriers, minimizing water decomposition by-products.

Zeta potential measurements also revealed the mechanism's effectiveness. In Fig. 10 b, FCDs reached −26.5 mV in distilled water due to their abundant negative groups (−SO3 −, –COO−, –OH). Moreover, upon incorporation into ZnSO4 electrolyte, FCDs shifted to −7.65 mV, indicating strong Zn2+ binding (Fig. 10 c).

Through the formation of its protective layer, the regulation of ion flux, and the suppression of side reactions, CDs can improve ZIB’s interface stability [174]. Cai et al. [175] duly used N, S-doped CDs (TU-CQDs) as electrolyte additives in KOH systems. Through a combination of experimental studies and three-dimensional transient nucleation modeling, such CDs showed how TU-CQDs' unique functional groups can modify electrode surface states and Zn species coordination behavior. The results were compelling. TU-CQDs are seen to inhibit hydrogen evolution reactions, accelerating uniform Zn deposition. In suppressing dendritic growth and enhancing Zn plating/stripping reversibility, in-situ synchrotron radiation X-ray images revealed their effectiveness. This work establishes a promising strategy for improving Zn metal anode reversibility in alkaline electrolytes, showcasing how strategic CD implementation can optimize multiple aspects of battery performance simultaneously.

While interface and electrolyte modifications have advanced, separator modification remains an underexplored strategy for bolstering ZIB performance [176]. Current AZIB separators: glass fiber [177], polymers [178], and cellulose [179] present distinct advantages and limitations in mechanical strength, hydrophilicity, and ion transport capabilities. Chen et al. [173] regulated Zn deposition behavior through CD-modified cellulose filter paper separators (FP-CQDs). In Fig. 10 d, these separators assisted uniform ion transport and maintained their structural integrity. In Fig. 10 e, ionic conductivity increased. In Fig. 10 f, under fast-charge conditions, superior cycling performance effectively suppressed the formation of dendrites (Fig. 10 g). Despite these promising results in CD-enhanced separators, particularly ion transport and dendrite suppression, many challenges, regards quantitative evaluation and mechanistic understanding, remain. To advance practical implementation in ZIB technology, future research should focus on scalable synthesis methods, degradation mechanisms, and cost-effectiveness.

The remarkable impact of CDs on ZIB performance came about due to the sophisticated interplay of mechanisms that transform battery functionality at multiple levels. The unique surface characteristics of CDs are to be found where abundant functional groups, strategic defects, and active edge sites orchestrate improvements in redox processes, interfacial wettability, and electrocatalytic activity [180]. Hybrid electrodes, particularly those combining CDs with metal substrates, represent a significant advancement in electrode design. Traditional approaches, using transition-metal oxides (MnO2, Ni(OH)2, Co3O4) or carbon-based materials (graphene, CNTs, carbon nanofibers), face inherent limitations [181]. Transition-metal oxides struggle with cycle life longevity while carbon-based materials exhibit lower capacitance [37,124,182].

Fig. 11 shows the integration of CDs in ZIBs. Functionalized with various surface groups, CDs enhance battery performance through multiple mechanisms: quantum confinement effects, interfacial engineering, electronic conduction enhancement, synergistic effects, and structural engineering. These mechanisms work together across battery components to improve ion transport, suppress dendrites, and enhance cycling stability. Through their multifaceted surface chemistry and structural versatility, CDs create synergistic effects that advance both performance and stability in ZIB technology.
The enhancement of zinc ion transport by CDs occurs through several concurrent mechanisms that operate at different spatial and temporal scales.

At the molecular level, CDs manage Zn ion transport through an elegant interplay of surface chemistry and structural design. Their surfaces, adorned with oxygen (–COOH, –OH), nitrogen, and sulfur functional groups, build preferential pathways for Zn2+ migration. Hence, diffusion barriers are strategically reduced. This sophisticated surface engineering is seen to optimize EDL at the electrode–electrolyte interfaces, accelerating charge separation and reducing interfacial resistance.

Xiong et al. [152] engineered nitrogen and sulfur co-doped CDs (NSCDs) with N species in the core and −SO3H groups on the outer shell (Fig. 12
a and b). DFT calculations revealed that these NSCDs’ acquired a remarkable affinity for Zn2+ ions, exhibiting stronger binding energies than water (Fig. 12 c and d). The results proved striking. After 200 cycles, NSCD-modified anodes maintained dense, uniform Zn deposition (Fig. 12 f). In contrast, the unmodified anodes showed significant degradation (Fig. 12 e). This work demonstrates how strategic CD surface engineering can simultaneously augment ion transport while suppressing parasitic reactions and dendrite formation, marking a significant advance in ZIB technology.

The structural modification of materials by CDs plays a pivotal role in enhancing ionic transport through multiple mechanisms. By creating hierarchical pore networks and optimizing pore size distribution, CDs can increase the effective surface area available for ion transport. CDs also function as molecular spacers between material layers, establishing efficient ion transport channels. Likewise, diffusion path lengths are reduced. These structural modifications, combined with their ability to modify grain boundaries, result in reduced interfacial resistance and enhanced intergranular ion transport.

Xiong et al. [147] undertook a compelling demonstration of these effects. The researchers synthesized NCDs, using EDTA-2Na through a solid-phase method. When combined with TiO2 as a Zn anode coating layer, the NCDs effectively suppressed dendrite formation and promoted uniform Zn deposition. This enhancement arose owing to the polar functional groups (–OH, –COOH, and –NH2) of NCDs, which provided abundant nucleation sites. In Fig. 12 g, CDs effectively regulated surface morphology through controlled content adjustment. In Fig. 12 h1-h4, over multiple cycles, a stark contrast is observed between random protrusions on the bare Zn and uniform deposition on Zn-TiO2/NCDs anodes. The resulting full cell achieved remarkable stability with 90 % capacity retention, over 1000 cycles.

Recently, CD applications have been extended to organic materials: notably with polyaniline (PANI) in ZIBs. Ye et al. [161] stated that incorporating edge-sulfonated graphene quantum dots (SGQDs) into PANI much improved discharge capacity and rate performance. This enhancement was attributed to interactions between SGQDs' −SO3 − groups and PANI's backbone structure. In the aqueous ZnSO4 electrolyte, the SGQDs/PANI composite exhibited exceptional stability, maintaining 81 % capacity after 1500 cycles. The composite substantially outperformed the unmodified PANI electrodes.

CDs effectively mitigate Zn dendrite formation through multiple synergistic mechanisms. By modifying surface energy dynamics and amplifying current distribution uniformity, CDs fundamentally alter the nucleation processes that govern Zn deposition. Surface modification promotes two-dimensional lateral growth over vertical dendritic formation. In the same way, uniform nucleation sites are created across the anode surface. Besides, CDs help achieve homogeneous current density distribution, eliminating localized “hot spots” that typically initiate dendrite growth.

The widespread adoption of ZIBs remains constrained by the formation of dendrites, particularly under lean-Zn conditions. For promoting horizontal Zn growth, crystalline lattice-induced deposition has shown promise. Yet, achieving consistent long-term regulation of Zn anodes continues to present significant challenges. In Fig. 13
a, Xu et al. [153] addressed this challenge through nitrogen and sulfur co-doped CDs (N,S-CDs) with graphene-like structures as electrolyte additives. The abundant electronegative functional groups of N, S-CDs effectively coordinate with Zn2+ ions, expediting controlled co-deposition at the anode surface (Fig. 13 b and c). This mechanism facilitates the parallel alignment of the (002) crystal plane, during Zn deposition. Hence, the probability of dendrite growth is substantially reduced (Fig. 13 d-f). Further, ion-beam bombardment analysis revealed enhanced carbon intensity signals from N, S-CD-modified Zn plates compared to unmodified samples. This outcome confirmed successful surface modification. Initially, it is seen that the unmodified samples showed higher carbon signals. These findings occurred due to increased surface area from vertical Zn microplate growth. The N, S-CD-modified surfaces, however, revealed consistent behavior, indicating their potential for sustained regulation of Zn deposition patterns. Results suggest that strategic deployment of functionalized CDs offers a promising approach for controlling Zn deposition morphology in next-generation battery systems.

Through the formation of stable SEI layers, CDs highly contribute to ZIB performance. In essence, SEI layers simultaneously protect against electrolyte decomposition and regulate Zn ion flux. Their ability to form coordination complexes with Zn ion modifies reduction kinetics. As a result, both deposition morphology and battery performance are regulated. Shaker et al. [183] demonstrated the benefits of SEI layers, using GQDs as electrolyte additives in 2 M ZnSO4. Their research revealed that GQDs selectively adsorb onto high-potential regions of Zn anodes through strong bonds between Zn2+ ions and oxygen-containing functional groups. In Fig. 13 g, this interaction is seen to reduce nucleation overpotential, promoting dendrite-free Zn deposition. Comparative analysis showed that while unmodified surfaces developed extensive sheet-like dendrites, GQD-modified surfaces exhibited remarkably smooth Zn plating layers (Fig. 13 h). The optimized Zn deposition uniformity stemmed from GQDs serving as nucleation sites. GQDs have lower electronegativity strengthening interactions with Zn2+ ions. These modifications result in reduced polarization voltage windows at various current densities. Hence, cell lifespans are greatly expanded compared to unmodified systems.

Wang et al. [149] demonstrated a novel approach to Zn anode protection in alkaline ZIBs, using negatively charged CDs. In their research, it was found that CDs can form a multifunctional barrier, providing both physical and chemical protection at the electrode interface (Fig. 13 i). This barrier selectively regulates ion transport, facilitating Zn ion transmission while effectively blocking zincate ions through electrostatic interactions. In Fig. 13 j, this selective transport mechanism achieved two vital objectives: optimizing electric field distribution for Zn ion deposition and preventing zincate ion accumulation at the electrode interface. The resulting dual-function protection system much improved the stability of Zn anodes and electrochemical performance. Such headway signifies an important advance in dendrite suppression strategies for ZIBs.

Significant interest has been directed toward suppressing adverse reactions and eliminating zinc dendrites to improve Zn anode performance. One of the most effective strategies to enhance reversibility and utilization efficiency is through controlled Zn ion deposition, achieved via surface coating protection and electrolyte modification. However, several challenges remain. The following provides a comprehensive overview of two key interfacial engineering strategies designed to mitigate dendrite formation and adverse effects.

During charging and discharging, zinc ions continuously deposit and dissolve on the anode surface. Dendrite formation arises from the uneven electric field distribution and the uncontrolled two-dimensional (2D) diffusion of Zn2+ ions at the anode-electrolyte interface [184] (Fig. 14 a). Initially, Zn ions accumulate at high-energy charge transfer sites, forming small bumps. Over time, additional Zn ions preferentially deposit at these sites to minimize surface energy, leading to the gradual growth of primary dendrites.

Under normal conditions, brittle, needle-shaped dendrites amplify the tip effect, acting as charge centers that further disrupt the electric field and accelerate dendritic growth [185]. Once dendrites reach a critical size, they pierce the separator, causing internal short circuits, severe capacity loss, and reduced cycle life. Additionally, fractured dendrites contribute to Zn depletion by generating “dead zinc,” further degrading electrochemical performance [186].

An effective strategy to enhance the stability and cycle life of Zn electrodes is the application of a protective layer on the anode surface. This layer acts as a physical barrier between the Zn anode and the electrolyte, suppressing dendrite formation and regulating Zn ion deposition. Additionally, it prevents direct Zn-electrolyte contact, mitigating adverse side reactions.

Electrolyte additives, when adsorbed onto the Zn anode, can modify surface interactions to form an electrostatic shielding layer, guiding Zn ions to deposit uniformly downward rather than accumulating at dendritic tips [188]. Based on studies of coating materials, four key mechanisms contribute to dendrite suppression at the anode interface: (1) confinement effects, (2) uniform interfacial electric field distribution, (3) enhanced nucleation sites, and (4) electrostatic shielding. These mechanisms collectively stabilize Zn deposition, improving battery performance and longevity.

The electrodeposition behavior of Zn ions can be effectively controlled using inorganic oxides with low conductivity and high dielectric constants. These materials limit Zn ion migration paths, particularly when incorporated into porous oxide coatings, which guide Zn deposition in an ordered, bottom-to-top fashion rather than randomly (Fig. 14b).

Additionally, inorganic salt coatings promote homogeneous Zn deposition by redistributing Zn ions and reducing localized ion concentration gradients near nucleation clusters [165]. By further regulating Zn plating rates, these coatings shape electrolyte flux through confinement effects, ensuring a uniform ion distribution. The nonporous structure and significant potential differences between the insulating coating and surrounding medium further suppress dendritic growth, enhancing Zn electrode stability.

The plating and stripping of Zn ions are fundamental to the electrochemical processes occurring on the Zn anode surface. Zn-ion deposition is primarily driven by the electric field, and its strength and uniformity directly influence nucleation and deposition behavior. For compact, controlled Zn nucleation and improved cycling stability, maintaining a uniform interfacial electric field is essential. However, disordered Zn deposition disrupts this field, leading to uneven growth and dendrite formation [165]. To counteract this, a highly conductive layer with a stable, uniform electric field effectively prevents local charge accumulation, promoting even Zn deposition and inhibiting dendritic growth (Fig. 14c).

When the nucleation overpotential is modest, Zn deposition typically begins at favorable nucleation sites. However, uneven Zn deposition can lead to dendritic growth. To achieve smooth Zn deposits and inhibit dendrite formation, artificially enhanced nucleation sites are introduced to promote uniform Zn deposition (Fig. 14d). The increase in active sites refines nucleation size, lowers nucleation overpotential, and facilitates uniform Zn growth and dissolution on the surface. Certain high-molecular polymer coatings, enriched with polar functional groups, exhibit zincophilic behavior due to their strong interactions with Zn ions. This property fosters homogeneous Zn deposition by providing an abundance of nucleation sites. The introduction of zincophilic sites, particularly nitrogen-functionalized sites, has been shown to significantly suppress dendrite formation. In model systems using carbon-based hosts, such as carbon dots (CDs), enhanced electrochemical performance and more uniform Zn deposition have been observed [189]. Additionally, the three-dimensional (3D) architecture of coating interfaces plays a crucial role in guiding Zn deposition and further improving its uniformity [190].

Functional additives create a flat surface, facilitate uniform Zn ion deposition, and effectively suppress Zn dendrites. One of the primary strategies to achieve this is the formation of an electrostatic shielding layer, which regulates Zn plating behavior and prevents localized ion accumulation. This shielding layer is established by ionic additives with a lower reduction potential, which preferentially adsorb onto Zn tips, altering the local electric field distribution. By mitigating the “tip effect,” these additives redirect Zn ions to deposit more uniformly downward rather than forming vertical dendrites, thereby enhancing cycling stability. As illustrated in (Fig. 14e), several dendrite suppression mechanisms contribute to this stabilization, including the confining effect, uniform electric field distribution, increased nucleation sites, electrostatic shielding, and induction of crystallographic orientation. These mechanisms collectively reduce Zn2+ ion concentration polarization and promote controlled deposition, ultimately prolonging the lifespan and efficiency of ZIBs [187].

Crystallographic orientation induction is a promising technique for enhancing the electrochemical performance of Zn anodes by controlling the crystallographic orientation of Zn deposition. The angle between the Zn deposition direction and the anode surface plays a critical role in dendrite formation (Fig. 14f). When this angle is large, particularly between 70° and 90°, dendritic growth is significantly promoted [191]. In contrast, orienting Zn deposition within a 0° to 30° range reduces dendrite formation and minimizes corrosion reactions by fostering a more compact and uniform Zn layer [188]. The crystalline structure of Zn, when aligned parallel to the current collector, facilitates stable Zn plating and stripping, improving long-term cycling stability in mild electrolytes [191]. Moreover, a well-controlled Zn deposition morphology minimizes the contact area between the Zn anode and electrolyte, reducing side reactions and successfully inhibiting dendritic growth and byproduct formation. These findings underscore the importance of crystallographic control in developing high-performance, long-lasting Zn anodes.

CDs enhance structural stability in ZIBs through multiple complementary mechanisms operating across different spatial scales. CD’s unique ability to modify interfaces, passivate defects, relieve mechanical stress, and establish cross-linking networks with other battery components can considerably improve overall structural integrity. During cycling, these modifications play an indispensable role in preventing material degradation, ultimately leading to increased performance and extended operational lifetime.

The effectiveness of CDs in structural stabilization is due to their versatile surface chemistry and nanoscale dimensions. Thus, CDs are able to simultaneously address multiple degradation pathways. Moreover, through careful interface engineering and defect management, CD modifications create resilient electrode architectures capable of withstanding the mechanical and chemical stresses associated with repeated charge–discharge cycles. This approach to structural reinforcement is seen to promote the development of more durable ZIB systems.

In ESSs, through multiple mechanisms, CDs augment the structural stability and longevity of active materials. Strong interfacial interactions between CDs and active materials bolster particle cohesion, effectively minimizing material pulverization [111] while reinforcing the overall structural framework. CD networks also create essential buffer spaces that accommodate volume changes through elastic deformation, maintaining structural integrity during cycling. Han et al. [159] reported this stabilization effect in addressing the dissolution challenges of Prussian Blue Analog cathodes through the development of a Bi2Se3/NCDs composite cathode (Fig. 15
a). Surface morphology analysis via SEM revealed that NCD incorporation much refined the Bi2Se3 cathode structure (Fig. 15 b). As a result, additional active reaction sites were generated. The enhanced structural stability translated directly to improved electrochemical performance, with the Bi2Se3/NCDs composite exhibiting superior specific capacity and rate capability compared to unmodified Bi2Se3 cathodes (Fig. 15 c). Such findings highlight the significant potential of strategic CD integration in advancing ZIB technology through enhanced material stability.

By mitigating undesirable side reactions and preventing active material dissolution, CDs play a crucial role in enhancing the chemical stability of ZIBs. During cycling, CD’s ability to control structural evolution helps maintain electrode morphology, porosity, and surface area, leading to improved long-term performance. Li et al. [156] showcased this stabilizing effect through the incorporation of chitosan-derived CDs into V2O5 nanobelt-based cathodes (Fig. 15 d). The CDs acted as structure-directing agents, facilitating the formation of distinctive one-dimensional CCDs@V2O5 nanobelts. Such architectures featured multi-dimensional charge transfer pathways along both radial and axial directions, much improving their electron transport capabilities (Fig. 15 e). Better structural organization resulted in higher capacities, as described by their electrochemical performance (Fig. 15 f). As observed, this work provides evidence that strategic CD incorporation can favorably modify material morphology, thus maintaining long-term structural stability.

The incorporation of CDs into electrode materials can boost electrical conductivity through several synergistic mechanisms. CDs are able to create conductive pathways within the electrode matrix, reducing overall resistance and facilitating electron transfer between active materials. Their surface functionalities are seen to foster ion–electron interactions. At the same time, the electrode structure is stabilized, maintaining conductivity during extended cycling. These combined effects help to improve the electrochemical performance and stability of battery systems.

In their work, Wu et al. [157] indicated the effectiveness of CD modification in enhancing electronic conductivity through a VO2 nanorod composite decorated with CQDs (Fig. 15 g). CQDs effectively facilitated Zn2+ ion adsorption, resulting in enhanced capacity and superior rate capability (Fig. 15 h). FT-IR spectroscopy confirmed strong interfacial interactions through C=O bond formation at 2360 cm−1 between CQDs and VO2 (Fig. 15 i). This outcome led to improved electrode/electrolyte compatibility and a 3.6-fold increase in ion diffusion coefficient. Such improvements emphasize the fundamental role of strategic CD incorporation in optimizing electrode performance. The modification, herein, also increased the specific surface area from 4.4 m2 g−1 to 18.7 m2 g−1 and enhanced electrolyte wettability, as revealed by reduced contact angles (53° vs. 114°, Fig. 15 j). Tauc's analysis is seen to signify that CD incorporation reduced the semiconductor bandgap from 1.76 eV to 1.09 eV, thereby, substantially improving electrical conductivity (Fig. 15 k and l).

The introduction of electronegative dopants profoundly influences the electronic properties of CDs through quantum confinement effects. While traditional doping strategies incorporate elements such as boron [192], phosphorus, nitrogen, and sulfur [193], their impact extends beyond simple chemical modification. These dopants can trigger fundamental changes in electronic structures, giving rise to sophisticated charge redistribution patterns among carbon atoms as well as point defects and active sites [194]. Such changes prove particularly valuable in mitigating parasitic reactions and achieving extended battery cycling life.

Recent investigations, employing sulfur-doped CDs (S-doped CDs) in a dimethylformamide (DMF)/water solvent blend with ZnSO4 electrolyte, have revealed remarkable quantum-level interactions [151]. In the research, advanced computational analysis recorded exceptionally high adsorption energy (−6.23 eV) between Zn ions and the −SO3 − groups on CDs, indicating strong Zn affinity and substantially reduced nucleation barriers (Fig. 16
a). During plating/stripping cycles, detailed surface characterization confirmed that these −SO3 − groups successfully promoted uniform and compact Zn deposition (Fig. 16 b and c). Through systematic EIS measurements, an optimal CD concentration of 20 % DMF electrolyte resulted (Fig. 16 d). This optimization revealed a delicate balance in the system. However, insufficient CD concentrations failed to provide adequate nucleation sites. Moreover, excessive concentrations increased electrolyte viscosity, thus impeding Zn2+ ion transport.

The quantum confinement effects in these doped CDs are revealed through several sophisticated mechanisms. Basically, heteroatom doping introduces new energy levels within an electronic band structure, effectively modulating the bandgap and enhancing charge transfer capabilities [195]. Strategic incorporation of electronegative dopants creates localized electronic states that serve as active sites for Zn ion coordination and electron transfer. This doping-induced charge redistribution generates regions of enhanced electron density, facilitating stronger interactions with Zn ions: uniform deposition patterns flourish. Per se, the modified electronic structure results in improved charge carrier mobility and reduced internal resistance, contributing to superior rate performance in battery applications.

These quantum effects translate directly into practical performance improvements in ZIBs. The modified electronic structure is seen to reduce energy barriers for Zn ion transport. At the same time, interfacial charge transfer kinetics are amplified. Engineered surface states contribute to improved stability at the electrode–electrolyte interface. On the other hand, optimized charge distribution promotes more uniform Zn deposition morphology. This deeper understanding of quantum effects provides vital insights for optimizing CD properties in battery applications. The ability to fine-tune electronic structure through strategic doping represents a powerful approach for augmenting the performance of next-generation ZIBs, offering pathways toward improved energy storage solutions.

Recent advances in CD applications for ZIBs have revealed complex synergistic interactions that extend beyond simple additive improvements in performance metrics. These synergistic effects transpire through the intricate interplay between electronic transport, ionic conductivity, and structural stability, creating self-reinforcing enhancement mechanisms that significantly improve overall battery performance. Such synergy emerges from the dual role of CDs in facilitating both electron and ion transport. The conductive networks established by CDs provide efficient pathways for electron transfer. Their surface functional groups further optimize ionic transport channels. Demonstration of these synergistic effects was achieved through hydrophilic, nanocrystalline CQDs as electrolyte additives [49]. In Fig. 16 e and f, the highly graphitized structure increased electronic conductivity. The abundant hydrophilic functional groups promoted uniform Zn deposition. Under long-term cycling, this dual enhancement reduced solid-electrolyte interface impedance and lowered polarization voltage (Fig. 16 g and h). Besides, the integration of CDs with other carbon-based materials generated additional active sites and improved electrocatalytic activity, resulting in enhanced storage capacity.

At the electrode–electrolyte interfaces, CDs create a sophisticated interplay between multiple enhancement mechanisms. Their surface functional groups modify local electric field distributions. Their nanostructured surfaces also produce uniform ion flux patterns. Simultaneously, the conductive networks can facilitate charge transfer across interfaces: protective layers prevent parasitic reactions. These combined improvements in ionic and electronic conductivity substantially reduce polarization and promote rate performance, representing vital advancements for high-efficiency energy storage applications.

It is noted that CD modification establishes a self-reinforcing cycle between structural stability and electronic performance. Likewise, improved structural integrity preserves conductive pathways. Maintained conductivity prevents local stress concentrations that could otherwise lead to material degradation. The superior cycling stability of CD-modified electrodes support this mutual reinforcement.

These synergistic effects are clearly observed in multiple performance metrics. Here, rate capability is accelerated. Cycling stability is improved, and performance is superior. The cumulative impact arises from simultaneous optimization of various performance-limiting factors rather than addressing individual constraints in isolation. Understanding these complex interactions contribute important insights for optimizing CD properties, which is reflected in the good progress of ZIB technology. This widespread enhancement through synergistic effects paves the way for developing next-generation ESSs.

The various mechanisms by which CDs boost ZIB performance are seen to operate synergistically, creating cumulative improvements in both rate capability and cycling stability. Understanding these interactions provides important insights for optimizing battery design and performance.

CDs can bolster battery performance through several complementary mechanisms. By their simultaneous improvement of ion transport and conductivity within electrode materials, CDs are seen to amplify power density and rate capability. Thus, batteries can thereby carry out rapid charge and discharge. The optimization of electrode–electrolyte interfaces through CD modification accelerates more efficient charge transfer processes. These enhancements work in concert with multiple stabilization mechanisms that ensure sustained performance over extended cycling periods. CDs can effectively extend battery lifespan by simultaneously suppressing side reactions, inhibiting dendrite formation, and maintaining structural integrity. This approach results in energy storage devices with superior long-term reliability and durability, addressing key challenges in current battery technology.

Optimizing CD properties necessitates a sophisticated understanding of the interplay between various performance-influencing mechanisms.
