A systematic approach must balance multiple factors, including photoluminescence [85], charge transport, and chemical reactivity to maintain overall functionality. For ZIB applications, careful surface functionalization of CDs can improve ion–electron transport dynamics while maintaining interfacial stability. Advanced optimization strategies, including experimental design techniques and computational modeling [196], enable comprehensive evaluation of performance-affecting factors [112].

The successful integration of CDs into battery systems demands careful consideration of implementation methods i.e. direct mixing, polymer matrix embedding, and layer-by-layer assembly. Each approach has distinct advantages and challenges, requiring careful assessment of CD-host material interactions to upgrade electrochemical properties. The maintenance of mechanical integrity is essential. Key challenges must prevent the agglomeration of CDs, managing phase separation, and avoiding adverse chemical reactions that could compromise long-term stability.

Understanding the fundamental mechanisms governing CD behavior is fundamental for optimizing battery component design. Such knowledge is paramount for strengthening CD-host material interactions and improving overall battery performance. Future research priorities should focus on elucidating these mechanisms, quantifying their performance, and developing predictive models to guide design decisions. These advances will be essential for maximizing the efficiency and durability of next-generation battery technologies through strengthened synergistic effects between CDs and other materials [197].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Diversity of alternative carbon materials in energy storage applications
ce:section.ce:para: Recently, substantial progress has been made in synthesizing carbon-based materials for energy storage applications, categorized by dimensionality into zero-dimensional (0D), one-dimensional (1D), two-dimensional (2D), and three-dimensional (3D) structures [198]. As shown in Fig. 17 , these include 0D carbon quantum dots, 1D carbon nanotubes, 2D graphene-based nanosheets, and 3D microporous or mesoporous carbon spheres, each possessing distinct nanostructural features. Additionally, materials such as activated carbon, which lack a well-defined structure, are widely utilized. The structural and morphological diversity of these materials significantly influences their electrochemical performance in battery and supercapacitor electrodes. For instance, 0D materials provide high surface areas, 1D materials enhance conductivity, 2D materials offer superior mechanical strength, and 3D structures facilitate efficient ion transport. Understanding these structure-performance relationships is crucial for advancing carbon-based energy storage technologies.

One-dimensional (1D) carbon materials, characterized by their elongated nanostructures, have gained considerable attention for supercapacitor applications due to their unique structural and electronic properties. These materials, including carbon nanotubes (CNTs), carbon nanofibers (CNFs), and graphene nanoribbons, exhibit a high aspect ratio, providing an extensive surface area and enabling rapid charge–discharge rates essential for high capacitance. Their exceptional mechanical strength also makes them suitable for applications under high-stress conditions. CNTs are classified into single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs) and multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) [200]. CNTs are widely regarded as promising electrode materials due to their large surface area, lightweight nature, excellent electrical conductivity, and intrinsic flexibility. CNFs also exhibit high conductivity, flexibility, and structural stability, making them ideal for energy storage applications. Their 1D structure enables efficient electron transport and short ion diffusion paths, enhancing electrochemical performance [201].

Two-dimensional (2D) carbon materials, such as graphene and porous carbon nanosheets (PCNs), possess large open-layer structures and high surface areas. These materials demonstrate excellent electronic transport properties and high aspect ratios, making them ideal for energy storage applications [202]. Unlike conventional porous materials, their high surface area is independent of pore distribution, thereby reducing ion-transport resistance and diffusion distances. These properties improve electrode kinetics, positioning 2D carbon materials as leading candidates for advanced energy storage systems.

Three-dimensional (3D) carbon materials, such as carbon spheres, are extensively studied and categorized into solid and hollow carbon spheres. These structures typically range in diameter from a few hundred nanometers to several micrometers. Among different morphologies, spherical carbon materials offer the highest surface area per unit mass, resulting in an increased number of active sites for electrochemical reactions [203]. This enhanced specific surface area is particularly advantageous for applications requiring high reactivity and efficient charge storage. Furthermore, carbon spheres exhibit superior physical and thermal stability compared to other morphologies, making them well-suited for demanding environments. Collectively, these properties position carbon spheres as promising candidates for advanced energy storage and conversion systems.
Table 4 provides a comparative analysis of multi-dimensional carbon materials used in ZIBs, highlighting key performance factors such as specific capacity and cycling stability.

Advances in CD research have demonstrated their huge potential in energy applications, particularly through structural design innovations and multifaceted functionality. These developments present unique opportunities for exploring novel phenomena and properties in multidisciplinary contexts. However, several critical challenges need to be addressed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Current limitations in CD synthesis and application
ce:section.ce:para: The advancement of CD technology faces many synthesis challenges. Foremost, among these, is developing scalable methods that consistently produce high-quality CDs with precise control over size, crystallinity, and defect distribution [207]. The relationship between purity and QY is poorly understood [208]. Impurities such as amorphous carbon and oligomers greatly affect optical and electronic properties. Bottom-up synthesis approaches, particularly those using citric acid precursors, frequently yield fluorophore contaminants that complicate structural control and limit energy storage applications. In addition, critical challenges include achieving narrow size distributions, as dimensional variations can impact optical and electronic behavior. Despite progress in producing multicolor emissive CDs [209], standardized methods for tuning band gaps [210,211] and photoluminescence are still lacking [212]. The underutilization of advanced characterization techniques, such as time-resolved electron paramagnetic resonance and fluorescence correlation spectroscopy, further hampers progress in understanding and optimizing CD properties.

The integration of CDs into ZIBs presents additional challenges, particularly regarding aggregation and dispersion within battery components. Maintaining long-term stability under harsh electrochemical conditions is dispensable. Degradation has a powerful effect on battery capacity and cycle life. Our understanding of the mechanisms by which CDs can increase ZIB performance continues to evolve, creating knowledge gaps that hinder optimization efforts.

Cost-effective, scalable production of high-quality CDs also presents challenges. Further development of efficient and environmentally friendly synthesis methods needs to be assessed [213]. Successful integration into existing battery manufacturing processes demands careful consideration of compatibility issues and potential side effects [214]. Addressing these issues is essential for realizing the full potential of CDs in advancing ZIB technology.

Despite their remarkable versatility and promising properties, the full potential of CDs in industrial applications remains unrealized due to significant challenges associated with scalability. Furthermore, identification of optimal synthesis methods and the scalability of green-synthesized CDs are most challenging. Owing to the inherent heterogeneity of natural precursors as well as the complexity of the associated synthesis and purification processes, their development is hindered. Conventional fabrication techniques, such as hydrothermal, solvothermal, and microwave-assisted approaches, are often characterized by high energy consumption, lengthy processing times, or stringent reaction parameter control. These factors collectively restrict their suitability for bulk production [215].

Scaling up production of CDs is a significant challenge due to the intricate synthesis techniques and low yield associated with conventional methods. To address this limitation, Li et al. [216] achieved a remarkable breakthrough by synthesizing 1.49 kg of CDs in a single batch within just 2 h, utilizing a one-step hydrothermal method with poplar leaves, as the precursor. Further, Yang et al. [217] successfully synthesized 120 g of CDs in a single batch, using chinese ink, as a precursor. Although carbon-rich materials are ideal precursors for large-scale CD synthesis, the extensive use of strong acids in these processes raises huge environmental concerns. As a more sustainable alternative, biomass and molecular precursors offer considerable potential on account of their abundance and diversity. For instance, Sahu et al. [218] utilized orange juice and ethanol to produce 0.4 g of CDs through hydrothermal treatment at 120 °C for 2.5 h.

This approach represents a substantial advancement in scalable production of CDs, offering a practical pathway for their large-scale fabrication. Such developments are important for enabling the widespread application of CDs across various disciplines. Achieving consistent physicochemical properties across batches presents a critical hurdle. Size uniformity, surface functionalization, and the optical characteristics of CDs can influence their performance in industrial settings. However, current synthesis routes often struggle to maintain consistent control over these parameters across different batches. Additionally, the use of expensive precursors and solvents, coupled with the generation of by-products in certain pathways, further complicate scalability and raise environmental concerns [219].

To address these issues, the focus is now on shifting towards the development of “green” and scalable synthesis methods [220]. One promising approach involves the utilization of biomass-derived precursors [221]. These methods leverage inexpensive and renewable raw materials, thereby minimizing the environmental footprint of CD production. Advancements in continuous flow reactors and the implementation of automated processes also offer potential solutions to overcome scalability bottlenecks. Nevertheless, achieving a balance between cost, quality control, and environmental sustainability remains crucial for the widespread industrial adoption of CDs. To optimize these factors and unlock the full potential of CDs in various industrial applications, continued innovation is necessary.

The successful integration of CDs into ZIBs needs to address several interconnected challenges through innovative synthesis approaches. A primary focus lies in developing precise control over CD parameters, such as particle size, surface functionality, and crystallinity. This control can be achieved through advanced methodologies: continuous flow techniques and green chemistry practices.

Optimization of CD integration into battery components presents another key challenge, requiring careful consideration of dispersion characteristics, interfacial compatibility, and long-term stability. The development of sustainable and scalable production processes is essential for establishing commercial viability. These processes must balance manufacturing efficiency with environmental considerations while maintaining precise control over CD properties.

Success in dealing with these challenges will advance our ability to harness CD potential in next-generation ESSs. The advancement of CDs for ZIB applications demands targeted material development strategies, addressing multiple technical challenges. A key focus involves designing task-specific CDs with precisely controlled surface functionalization to optimize interfacial compatibility and electrochemical performance. The development of smart responsive CDs presents promising solutions to integration challenges, potentially improving stability and dispersion characteristics within battery components [222].

Hybrid materials represent another promising direction, particularly CD-polymer composites and metal-CD hybrids [223]. These multi-component systems leverage synergistic interactions between constituent materials to overcome performance limitations. Applying these hybrid materials can bring about long-term stability across various operational environments. To elucidate critical structure–property relationships, sophisticated characterization approaches, including in-situ and operando studies combined with advanced microscopy techniques, are required [224,225]. The integration of these innovative material development strategies proffers a broad scope in facing current limitations in CD-enhanced ZIBs. Success in these efforts will significantly advance both the performance and sustainability of next-generation energy storage technologies.

Advancing CD integration in ZIBs demands a balanced approach, combining fundamental studies with applied research. Primarily, investigations should focus on elucidating mechanisms governing CD-battery component interactions and understanding degradation processes that affect long-term stability. These insights are vital for optimizing CD properties and predicting performance characteristics.

Parallel applied research efforts must also focus on translating theoretical understanding into practical applications. Key priorities include developing seamless integration methods for existing battery architectures, establishing rigorous protocols for validating performances, thus ensuring reliability across diverse operating conditions. Future development should emphasize three critical areas: 1. Scalable synthesis methods enabling widespread adoption, 2. Enhanced performance metrics demonstrating clear CD advantages, and 3. Cost-effective production practices, ensuring commercial viability.

Sustainability considerations must underpin all development efforts, aligning with global environmental standards. Such an approach to research and development will help overcome current technical challenges while unlocking the transformative potential of CDs in next-generation energy storage solutions.

CDs have demonstrated significant potential in advanced AZIBs due to their high surface area, superior conductivity, and intrinsic stability. This review underscores recent advancements in CD applications for AZIBs and their role in improving electrochemical performance. However, challenges such as scalability, stability, and interfacial interactions must be addressed to fully realize their potential.

Large-scale synthesis of CDs using renewable and environmentally friendly resources is critical for sustainable development. Current synthetic approaches remain largely confined to laboratory settings, and their scalability to industrial production is uncertain. Structural integrity, aggregation, and stability in aqueous environments further impact battery efficiency and cycle life. Future research should explore scalable green synthesis techniques and process optimization to maintain CD properties in industrial production.

Key issues include optimizing CD processing techniques, enhancing interfacial interactions, and mitigating degradation during cycling. Effective electrode fabrication, precise tuning of electrochemical properties, and improved electrolyte compatibility will be essential in addressing these challenges. Experimental studies should focus on understanding charge transfer mechanisms, electrolyte-CD interactions, and strategies to suppress zinc dendrites for improved cycling stability.

To advance commercialization, collaborative efforts between academia and industry are crucial. Pilot-scale studies with battery manufacturers should be initiated to evaluate real-world performance and economic feasibility. Additionally, regulatory assessments should ensure sustainability and compliance with industrial standards. Advancements in high-throughput screening and AI-driven material design could accelerate the discovery of next-generation CDs with tailored properties for AZIBs.

By overcoming these challenges through continued research, strategic planning, and industrial collaboration, CD-enhanced AZIBs can emerge as a key solution for next-generation energy storage, supporting the transition towards a sustainable energy future.
